Title: Single vs Repeat Surgical Skin Preparations for Reducing Surgical Site Infection After Total Joint Arthroplasty: A Prospective, Randomized, Double-Blinded Study.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Preventing surgical site infection (SSI) after total joint arthroplasty (TJA) is a high priority and is partly linked to the efficacy of surgical site preparation solutions (SPSs) in reducing the number of pathogens on the skin before incision. The aim of this study is to investigate the effectiveness of SRS reapplication after draping to reduce the incidence of SSI after TJA.

METHODS: Six hundred patients undergoing primary TJA between 2010 and 2011 at a single institution were recruited and randomly assigned to one of 2 groups. The patients in the intervention group (nВ = 300) received SPS that included alcohol and povidone-iodine before draping and an additional SPS by iodine povacrylex and isopropyl alcohol before application of the final adhesive drape, whereas the patients in the control group (nВ = 300) received a single SPS with alcohol and povidone-iodine before draping. Randomization was performed by an opaque envelope, and the rates of SSI and blistering were compared between groups.

RESULTS: Five seventy-seven patients completed the study and were included in the final analysis. There was a significant reduction in the incidence of superficial SSI for the intervention group (1.8%, 5 of 283) compared to the control group (6.5%, 19 of 294, PВ = .02). There were 2 (0.7%, 2 of 294) deep incisional SSIs in the control group, and 2 (0.7%, 2 of 283) organ-space SSIs in the intervention group (PВ = 1.00). In addition, skin blistering was lower in the intervention group (3.5%, 10 of 283) vs the control group (6.5%, 19 of 294), but this difference also did not reach statistical significance (PВ = .13).

CONCLUSION: Reapplication of an SPS after draping and before the application of iodophor-impregnated incisive draping resulted in a significant reduction in the rate of SSI in patients undergoing elective TJA.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a kid-friendly explanation:

This study was about finding better ways to prevent infections after people have surgery on their joints (like hip or knee replacements). When doctors do surgery, they need to make sure the skin is super clean to prevent germs from causing infections.

The doctors tested two different ways of cleaning the skin:
1. One group of patients got their skin cleaned once before surgery
2. Another group got their skin cleaned twice - once before and once after they put special surgical covers (called drapes) on the patient

They found that cleaning the skin twice worked better! The patients who got their skin cleaned twice had fewer infections than those who only got cleaned once. They also had slightly fewer skin problems like blisters, though this difference wasn't as significant.

Think of it like washing your hands - sometimes washing them once isn't enough to get rid of all the germs, and washing them twice does a better job. The same idea worked for keeping surgical patients safer from infections.

The main message is: cleaning the skin twice before surgery helps prevent infections better than cleaning it just once.

Title: Single vs Repeat Surgical Skin Preparations for Reducing Surgical Site Infection After Total Joint Arthroplasty: A Prospective, Randomized, Double-Blinded Study.
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Preventing surgical site infection (SSI) after total joint arthroplasty (TJA) is a high priority and is partly linked to the efficacy of surgical site preparation solutions (SPSs) in reducing the number of pathogens on the skin before incision.

METHODS: Six hundred patients undergoing primary TJA between 2010 and 2011 at a single institution were recruited and randomly assigned to one of 2 groups. The patients in the intervention group (n = 300) received SPS that included alcohol and povidone-iodine before draping and an additional SPS by iodine povacrylex and isopropyl alcohol before application of the final adhesive drape, whereas the patients in the control group (n = 300) received a single SPS with alcohol and povidone-iodine before draping. Randomization was performed by an opaque envelope, and the rates of SSI and blistering were compared between groups. The primary outcome was the incidence of SSI.

RESULTS: Five seventy-seven patients completed the study and were included in the final analysis. The incidence of superficial SSI was 1.8% (5 of 283) in the intervention group compared to 6.5% (19 of 294) in the control group (P = .02). There were 2 (0.7%, 2 of 294) deep incisional SSIs in the control group, and 2 (0.7%, 2 of 283) organ-space SSIs in the intervention group (P = 1.00). Skin blistering was observed in 3.5% (10 of 283) of the intervention group and 6.5% (19 of 294) of the control group (P = .13).

CONCLUSION: The treatment with reapplication of an SPS after draping was not more effective than a single application of SPS in reducing the overall incidence of SSI in patients undergoing elective TJA.

Title: Comparison of Treatments for Superior Labrum-Biceps Complex Lesions With Concomitant Rotator Cuff Repair: A Prospective, Randomized, Comparative Analysis of Debridement, Biceps Tenotomy, and Biceps Tenodesis.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes in patients with concomitant superior labrum-biceps complex (SLBC) lesions and rotator cuff tears who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, according to 3 different treatment methods (simple debridement, biceps tenotomy, or biceps tenodesis) for the SLBC lesions.

METHODS: One hundred twenty patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with SLBC lesions (biceps partial tears <50%, partial pulley lesions, and type II SLAP lesions) were enrolled in this prospective comparative study and randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups (simple debridement [Deb], biceps tenotomy only [BTo], or biceps tenodesis with one suture anchor [BTd]). Patients with isolated subscapularis tears or osteoarthritis were excluded. Finally, 86 patients (Deb in 28, BTo in 27, and BTd in 31) were analyzed (mean follow-up, 22.1 ± 7.72 months; mean age, 58.98 ± 7.8 years). The primary outcome was pain. Functional, clinical, and radiologic outcomes, as well as the strength index of elbow flexion and forearm supination, were also analyzed.

RESULTS: Pain, range of motion, and functional scores improved postoperatively in all 3 groups, with no significant differences across groups. At the 6-month follow-up, 8 patients reported cramping pain (5 Deb patients, 2 BTo patients, and 1 BTd patient). Bicipital groove tenderness improved in the BTo group postoperatively (P = .006). The Popeye deformity was noted in 10 patients (37.0%) in the BTo group, 8 (25.8%) in the BTd group, and 2 (7.1%) in the Deb group (P = .029). On radiologic examination, the presence of tenodesis of the biceps tendons on the bicipital groove showed low agreement with the presence of the Popeye deformity (observed in 13 of 20 patients with the deformity, 65%). On strength index analysis, the BTo group showed lower forearm supination strength (0.877 ± 0.44) compared with that in the other groups (1.29 ± 0.525 in the Deb group and 1.12 ± 0.451 in the BTd group, P = .049).CONCLUSIONS: The treatment methods did not show significant differences in improving pain and function in patients with SLBC lesions and rotator cuff tears. The treatment A was not more effective than comparator B in patients with these conditions.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a kid-friendly explanation:

This study looked at different ways to fix shoulder injuries where two parts are hurt at the same time: the rotator cuff (muscles that help you move your shoulder) and something called the biceps tendon (the muscle in your upper arm).

Doctors tried three different ways to fix these injuries:
1. Just cleaning up the damaged area
2. Cutting the damaged tendon
3. Reattaching the tendon in a new spot

They wanted to see which way worked best to help people feel better and use their shoulder normally again.

The interesting thing they found was that all three ways helped people feel better and move their shoulder better. However, some people who had their tendon cut (method 2) weren't quite as strong when turning their palm up, and some got a funny-looking bump in their arm (called a "Popeye muscle" because it looks like the cartoon character's big bicep).

The main message is that all three ways of fixing the shoulder worked pretty well for making the pain go away and helping people use their shoulder again, but each method had its own small advantages and disadvantages.

Title: Comparison of Treatments for Superior Labrum-Biceps Complex Lesions With Concomitant Rotator Cuff Repair: A Prospective, Randomized, Comparative Analysis of Debridement, Biceps Tenotomy, and Biceps Tenodesis.
Journal: Arthroscopy: The Journal of Arthroscopic & Related Surgery
Spin in Abstract Conclusion: "Trend toward significance" // "Numerically longer survival" or equivalent verbiage, Claim benefit based on (+) subgroup analysis
abstract:
PURPOSE: To compare the clinical outcomes in patients with concomitant superior labrum-biceps complex (SLBC) lesions and rotator cuff tears who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, according to 3 different treatment methods (simple debridement, biceps tenotomy, or biceps tenodesis) for the SLBC lesions.

METHODS: One hundred twenty patients who underwent arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with SLBC lesions (biceps partial tears <50%, partial pulley lesions, and type II SLAP lesions) were enrolled in this prospective comparative study and randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups (simple debridement [Deb], biceps tenotomy only [BTo], or biceps tenodesis with one suture anchor [BTd]). Patients with isolated subscapularis tears or osteoarthritis were excluded. Finally, 86 patients (Deb in 28, BTo in 27, and BTd in 31) were analyzed (mean follow-up, 22.1 В± 7.72 months; mean age, 58.98 В± 7.8 years). Pain; functional, clinical, and radiologic outcomes; and the strength index of elbow flexion and forearm supination were analyzed.

RESULTS: Pain, range of motion, and functional scores significantly improved postoperatively in all 3 groups, with no significant differences across groups. At the 6-month follow-up, 8 patients reported cramping pain (5 Deb patients, 2 BTo patients, and 1 BTd patient), but the pain improved in most patients over time (with cramping pain reported by zero Deb patients, 1 BTo patient, and 1 BTd patient at final follow-up). Bicipital groove tenderness significantly improved in the BTo group postoperatively (PВ = .006). The Popeye deformity was noted in 10 patients (37.0%) in the BTo group, 8 (25.8%) in the BTd group, and only 2 (7.1%) in the Deb group (PВ = .029). On radiologic examination, the presence of tenodesis of the biceps tendons on the bicipital groove showed low agreement with the presence of the Popeye deformity (observed in 13 of 20 patients with the deformity, 65%). On strength index analysis, the BTo group showed lower forearm supination strength (0.877 В± 0.44) compared with that in the other groups (1.29 В± 0.525 in the Deb group and 1.12 В± 0.451 in the BTd group, PВ = .049).CONCLUSIONS: All 3 treatments improved pain and function. Simple debridement showed the lowest risk of the Popeye deformity and preserved forearm supination strength. Biceps tenotomy and tenodesis may be preferable for selected patients: biceps tenotomy for patients with definite bicipital groove tenderness and biceps tenodesis for patients, especially male patients, with bicipital groove tenderness who want to preserve supination strength.

Here's a kid-friendly explanation:

This is a study about shoulder injuries and different ways to fix them. The researchers looked at people who had two problems in their shoulder: a torn rotator cuff (muscles that help you move your arm) and problems with another part called the biceps (the muscle that makes a bump when you flex your arm).

They tried three different ways to fix these shoulders:
1. Just cleaning up the damaged area
2. Cutting the biceps loose
3. Reattaching the biceps in a new spot

They wanted to see which way worked best. Here's what they found:
- All three ways helped reduce pain and helped people move their arms better
- The first method (just cleaning) had the least chance of making the arm look funny
- The second method (cutting the biceps) was good for people who had specific pain in one area
- The third method (reattaching) was good for people, especially men, who wanted to keep their arm strength for turning their palm up and down

Think of it like fixing a broken toy - sometimes you just need to clean it, sometimes you need to remove a broken part, and sometimes you need to reattach parts in a new way. Different methods work better for different people!

Title: Liposomal bupivacaine versus indwelling interscalene nerve block for postoperative pain control in shoulder arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Pain management strategies following shoulder arthroplasty vary significantly. Liposomal bupivacaine (LB) is an extended-release delivery of a phospholipid bilayer encapsulating bupivacaine that can result in drug delivery up to 72 hours. Prior studies in lower extremity surgery demonstrated efficacy of LB in comparison to a single-shot peripheral nerve block; however, no study has investigated LB in a total shoulder arthroplasty population. Therefore, this study compared LB vs. an indwelling interscalene nerve block (IINB).

METHODS: This is a prospective, randomized study of 83 consecutive shoulder arthroplasty patients; 36 patients received LB and a "bridge" of 30вЂ‰mL of 0.5% bupivacaine, and 47 patients received an IINB. Postoperative visual analog scale pain levels, opiate consumption measured with oral morphine equivalents, length of hospital stay, and postoperative complications were recorded. Continuous variables were compared using an analysis of variance with significance set at PвЂ‰<вЂ‰.05.

RESULTS: Visual analog scale pain scores were statistically higher in the LB cohort immediately postoperatively in the postanesthesia care unit (7.25 vs. 1.91; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000) as well as for the remainder of postoperative day 0 (4.99 vs. 3.20; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.005) but not for the remainder of admission. Opiate consumption was significantly higher among the LB cohort in the postanesthesia care unit (31.79 vs. 7.47; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000), on postoperative day 0 (32.64 vs. 15.04; PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000), and for the total hospital admission (189.50 vs. 91.70, PвЂ‰=вЂ‰.000). Complication numbers and length of stay were not statistically different.

CONCLUSION: Use of an IINB provides superior pain management in the immediate postoperative setting as demonstrated by decreased narcotic medication consumption and lower subjective pain scores.
plain_language_summary:
Here's a kid-friendly summary:

Scientists were trying to figure out the best way to help people deal with pain after shoulder surgery. They tested two different methods:

1. A special long-lasting medicine (LB) that slowly releases pain medication for up to 3 days
2. A thin tube placed near the shoulder that continuously gives pain medicine (IINB)

They tested these methods on 83 people who had shoulder surgery. Here's what they found:

- The people who got the tube (IINB) felt much less pain right after surgery
- These people also needed less strong pain medicine
- Both groups stayed in the hospital for about the same amount of time
- Both methods were equally safe

The final conclusion was that the tube method (IINB) worked better at controlling pain, especially right after the surgery, and people needed fewer pain pills when using this method.

Think of it like this: It's like comparing two ways to water a plant - using a slow-release water globe versus a small drip system. The scientists found that the drip system (like the tube) worked better at keeping the plant (or in this case, managing pain) happy!

Title: Liposomal bupivacaine versus indwelling interscalene nerve block for postoperative pain control in shoulder arthroplasty: a prospective randomized controlled trial.
Journal: Journal of Shoulder and Elbow Surgery
abstract:
BACKGROUND: Pain management strategies following shoulder arthroplasty vary significantly. Liposomal bupivacaine (LB) is an extended-release delivery of a phospholipid bilayer encapsulating bupivacaine that can result in drug delivery up to 72 hours. This study compared LB vs. an indwelling interscalene nerve block (IINB).

METHODS: This is a prospective, randomized study of 83 consecutive shoulder arthroplasty patients; 36 patients received LB and a "bridge" of 30 mL of 0.5% bupivacaine, and 47 patients received an IINB. The primary outcome was postoperative visual analog scale pain levels. Opiate consumption measured with oral morphine equivalents, length of hospital stay, and postoperative complications were also recorded. Continuous variables were compared using an analysis of variance with significance set at P < .05.

RESULTS: Visual analog scale pain scores were statistically higher in the LB cohort immediately postoperatively in the postanesthesia care unit (7.25 vs. 1.91; P = .000) as well as for the remainder of postoperative day 0 (4.99 vs. 3.20; P = .005) but not for the remainder of admission. Opiate consumption was significantly higher among the LB cohort in the postanesthesia care unit (31.79 vs. 7.47; P = .000), on postoperative day 0 (32.64 vs. 15.04; P = .000), and for the total hospital admission (189.50 vs. 91.70, P = .000). Complication numbers and length of stay were not statistically different.

CONCLUSION: The treatment with liposomal bupivacaine was not more effective than an indwelling interscalene nerve block in patients with shoulder arthroplasty regarding postoperative pain management.

1. A special long-lasting medicine called liposomal bupivacaine (LB) that slowly releases pain medicine for up to 3 days
2. A tiny tube placed near the shoulder that continuously gives pain medicine (called a nerve block)

They tested these methods on 83 people who had shoulder surgery. Here's what they found out:

- The people who got the long-lasting medicine (LB) felt more pain right after surgery

- Both groups stayed in the hospital for about the same amount of time
- Both groups had about the same number of problems or complications

The final conclusion: The special long-lasting medicine wasn't better than the tiny tube method for controlling pain after shoulder surgery. In fact, the tiny tube method worked better for immediate pain relief.

Think of it like comparing two ways to water a plant: using a slow-release water globe versus a small drip system. In this case, the drip system (nerve block) worked better than the slow-release method.
